subtitles.text: One island.
Two nations.
One buried history.
This is Hispaniola, known to its first peoples as Quisqueya, Ayiti,
and Bohío.
Over the next few minutes we'll trace why Haiti is the way it is: the Taíno world,
the West African crossing, Spanish and French rule, colorism,
two revolutions, the buried ransom of eighteen twenty-five,
and the road to today.
First, how to read this.
History this charged usually gets flattened into either patriotic legend or conspiracy theory,
so we'll sort everything into three buckets.
Established fact, documented in the record.
Contested claims, where real evidence sits on more than one side.
And myth, folklore dressed up as fact.
We'll name which is which as we go.
Long before the first European sail,
Arawak-speaking peoples migrated up the island chain from South America and built complex,
interconnected societies across the Greater Antilles.
They organized into five chiefdoms, farmed cassava and maize,
and gave the world words we still use: hurricane, barbecue, canoe,
hammock.
This island was densely populated and organized.
It was not empty.
Columbus made landfall in fourteen ninety-two and claimed the island for Spain.
Within about fifty years the Taíno population collapsed,
destroyed by Old World disease,
forced labor under the encomienda system, violence, and starvation.
To replace the dying,
Spain began shipping enslaved Africans here as early as fifteen oh one.
The friar Bartolomé de las Casas recorded the destruction firsthand.
There is nothing natural about a border on this island.
As Spain's interest drifted to the silver of the mainland,
French settlers took the under-defended west.
In sixteen ninety-seven,
Spain formally ceded that western third to France.
Two empires, two languages, two legal codes, two economies,
seeded on a single island.
Almost everything downstream flows from this one cut.
By the late seventeen hundreds,
French Saint-Domingue was one of the most profitable colonies on earth,
producing a vast share of the world's sugar and coffee and financing the ports of France.
But that wealth ran on the mass importation and mass death of enslaved Africans.
Roughly five hundred thousand enslaved people outnumbered whites about sixteen to one,
and mortality was so brutal the population could not reproduce itself.
Saint-Domingue did not inherit colorism.
It engineered it as a tool of control.
Colonial administrators built an obsessively ranked caste pyramid: whites at the top,
free people of color in the middle, the enslaved majority at the base,
precisely so the groups below could never unite against the planters.
Remember this template.
The same hierarchy returns,
later weaponized as anti-Haitian ideology next door.
In August seventeen ninety-one, a Vodou ceremony at Bois Caïman,
led by Boukman Dutty and Cécile Fatiman, lit the fuse.
Within days the northern plains were ablaze.
Out of the revolt rose Toussaint Louverture, born enslaved,
who forged a disciplined army that outmaneuvered Spain, Britain,
and France.
To keep Black soldiers fighting for the Republic,
France abolished slavery in seventeen ninety-three and ninety-four.
This is the only fully successful slave revolution in history.
In eighteen oh two,
Napoleon sent a massive expedition to crush the colony and restore slavery.
Toussaint was seized under a flag of truce and died in a French prison.
But the betrayal only hardened the resistance under Jean-Jacques Dessalines.
After the decisive victory at Vertières,
independence was declared on January first, eighteen oh four,
the first nation founded by formerly enslaved people.
In early eighteen oh four,
Dessalines ordered the killing of most remaining French settlers,
an act of revolutionary terror the Atlantic slave powers would never forgive.
To the slaveholding empires, and a slaveholding United States,
a republic of self-liberated Africans was not an inspiration.
It was a contagion to be contained.
Haiti was diplomatically and economically quarantined for decades,
shut out of trade and denied recognition.
The United States,
fearing it would inspire its own enslaved population,
withheld recognition until eighteen sixty-two,
during its own Civil War.
This is the single biggest answer.
In July eighteen twenty-five,
fourteen French warships arrived off Port-au-Prince.
France would recognize Haiti's independence on one condition: that Haiti pay one hundred fifty million gold francs to compensate former slaveholders for their lost property,
including the people themselves.
It was not a negotiation.
President Boyer signed.
To begin paying, Haiti was forced to borrow from French banks,
in a loan led by the financier Jacques Laffitte.
The sum was later cut to ninety million francs in eighteen thirty-eight,
and France received its last indemnity payment in eighteen eighty-eight.
But the loans taken to cover it, the so-called double debt,
were not finally cleared until around nineteen forty-seven.
For over a century,
national revenue flowed out to Paris instead of into schools, roads,
and hospitals.
For two hundred years it was barely told.
In twenty twenty-two,
the New York Times documented it and published the data openly.
Meanwhile, in the east, the piece most people miss.
The Dominican Republic did not win its independence from a European power.
It won independence from Haiti.
After a brief ephemeral independence in eighteen twenty-one,
Haitian president Boyer marched east and unified the entire island from eighteen twenty-two to eighteen forty-four.
That unification abolished slavery across the formerly Spanish east,
a real emancipation.
But Dominican elites experienced it as occupation,
through heavy taxation and forced service.
Both tellings contain truth.
In the east,
a young liberal named Juan Pablo Duarte returned from Europe determined to free his homeland.
In eighteen thirty-eight he founded La Trinitaria,
a clandestine independence movement organized in cells,
using theater troupes as cover for the conspiracy.
On February twenty-seventh, eighteen forty-four,
patriots seized the Puerta del Conde in Santo Domingo and the Dominican Republic was born.
Duarte wanted a sovereign republic with rights and elections,
and he opposed handing it to any empire.
Remember that.
A popular story imagines secret letters between Duarte and Spain.
The documented record is almost the reverse.
Duarte's secrecy was about independence.
The real secret dealing, to hand the country back to a foreign crown,
was run by his rivals, against him.
In eighteen forty-four,
the cattle-baron Pedro Santana seized power and exiled Duarte within months.
And in eighteen sixty-one,
Santana secretly re-annexed the Dominican Republic to Spain,
the only Latin American nation to return itself to colonial rule.
Dominicans rose up, and Spain was driven out by eighteen sixty-five.
The twentieth-century tyrants on each side did not appear from nowhere,
but they rose by different paths.
In the Dominican Republic,
Rafael Trujillo climbed the National Guard that U.S.
Marines built during the occupation,
then seized power and made anti-Haitianism and whitening state ideology,
ruling until his assassination in nineteen sixty-one.
In Haiti, François Duvalier, Papa Doc,
was elected in nineteen fifty-seven and then built his own power base,
sidelining the army with his Tontons Macoutes militia.
He and his son, Baby Doc, ran a family kleptocracy,
tolerated by Washington as a Cold War anti-communist bulwark.
In October nineteen thirty-seven, over roughly a week,
Trujillo's army systematically murdered Haitians,
and dark-skinned Dominicans, along the border.
Soldiers reportedly held up a sprig of parsley and demanded its Spanish word,
perejil.
Those whose accent failed the test were killed,
though historians debate how systematically that parsley test was really used.
Machetes were used so the state could pass it off as a spontaneous peasant uprising,
and a press blackout followed.
Estimates of the dead range from several thousand to tens of thousands,
the uncertainty itself a legacy of the cover-up.
The United States occupied Haiti from nineteen fifteen to nineteen thirty-four.
Behind the story was money.
A New York bank, National City Bank, today Citibank,
had already maneuvered into control of Haiti's national bank, and U.S.
forces had shipped Haiti's gold reserve to New York in nineteen fourteen.
Under U.S.
supervision,
a new constitution let foreigners own Haitian land for the first time,
overturning a core principle of eighteen oh four.
Revived forced labor sparked the Cacos rebellion.
The Marine general who led the occupation later called himself a gangster for capitalism.
Today the gap between the two countries is stark, and a common,
comfortable explanation blames Haitian culture.
That is a colorist trope, and it is wrong.
The real drivers are historical and structural,
and they were done to Haiti far more than chosen by it: the eighteen twenty-five ransom and debt trap,
decades of diplomatic isolation, foreign occupation and extraction,
predatory dictatorship,
and disaster exposure that is a symptom of poverty, not its cause.
That does not absolve Haiti's own elites or its worst policy choices,
which compounded the damage.
But the foundation was laid from outside.
So what was actually hidden,
and what does the evidence support? The eighteen twenty-five ransom: now documented,
reconstructed payment by payment in twenty twenty-two.
The nineteen thirty-seven massacre cover-up: now documented through survivor testimony and scholarship.
The cholera epidemic introduced by UN peacekeepers in twenty ten,
which killed around ten thousand Haitians: acknowledged by the UN in twenty sixteen.
And the contested one: the twenty oh four removal of President Aristide,
who had just demanded around twenty-one billion dollars in restitution from France.
He says the U.S.
and France forced him out.
Both deny it.
The timing fuels suspicion, but a coordinated plot remains unproven.
The modern unraveling came fast.
In twenty ten, a magnitude-seven earthquake flattened Port-au-Prince;
official estimates put the dead above two hundred thousand,
though the true toll is still disputed.
That same year, cholera, introduced by UN peacekeepers,
which killed nearly ten thousand people over the following years.
In twenty twenty-one,
President Jovenel Moïse was assassinated in his home and the state's authority unraveled.
By twenty twenty-four,
coordinated gangs had seized the capital and forced out the prime minister.
And in twenty twenty-six, a foreign security force deploys again,
with around one and a half million people displaced and no elections in over a decade.
So why is Haiti the way it is? It is not culture, and it is not race.
It is a structure produced over two hundred years,
each layer compounding the last.
Not one master plan,
but a chain of deliberate acts and hard consequences.
Revolution,
in which the enslaved freed themselves and terrified every slave power.
Quarantine, which cut the first Black republic off from trade.
The ransom, which locked in an unpayable debt trap.
Occupation, in which France's banks and then U.S.
Marines extracted and controlled.
Dictatorship, in which Cold-War-backed strongmen looted and divided.
And finally collapse, as disaster, weak institutions,
and gangs filled the vacuum.
And yet the people were never erased.
In twenty eighteen,
scientists sequenced an ancient Caribbean genome and confirmed genetic continuity with people living today.
Present-day Puerto Ricans, Dominicans,
and Cubans still carry Taíno ancestry.
The story was assimilation and collapse, not disappearance.
So hold onto three things.
One island, split by empires, not by nature.
Haiti's poverty was produced by history,
not by culture or race: revolution, to quarantine, to ransom,
to extraction.
And always separate fact from contested claim from myth,
especially online.
